content:
I think that when players first see this clash, they'll be confused about what "average mood" means, especially when you have a case like this:
Input--> :( (: ):
Output--> :|

Could you maybe describe in more detail what average mood/mean happiness means in the statement? Maybe it could just be me not understanding, but it would really help a lot.
Thank you!

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Simple input parsing puzzle that will surely make clashers :)

Currently, there is only 1 test and 2 validators that will output different emoticons depending on whether the happiness score is rounded or not. In Reverse mode, this becomes very unintuitive as an equally reasonable interpretation of this clash would be to count the difference between the number of smiley and sad faces. I would suggest disabling Reverse mode if the scoring is to be kept as the rounded mean happiness.

Also, just two minor tweaks: In the stub generator, please change "3" to "2". And, under the Constraints section, please add that each emoticon will be valid (one of the emoticons listed in the problem description). Thank you!

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: It seems to me to fit the passing criteria.
I found it ludic.
It exposed one of my lackings showing in fastest/reverse modes: reading the whole of the description(here I initially computed the total instead of the rounded mean)

Goal
* Examples should not replace formal specification. Consider revising the statement to explicitly state both orientations of each emoticon. "Space-separated" is also omitted in this section for simplification.

You are given a string of emoticons whose moods differ by expression. The happiness for each expression, in descending order, is shown below. Since people may lean either left or right, each expression can appear in two orientations.

+1 {{:)}} or {{(:}}
0 {{:|}} or {{|:}}
-1 {{:(}} or {{):}}

Input
* Please study the formatting syntax on this page: https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/40701/help-center/statement , and follow the presentation in the example there. In particular, add line numbers, variable types and descriptions where they're missing.

Constraints
* Please add the constraint for [[n]], specifying its lower and upper bounds.

Cases
* Current expected output distribution:
  • :) - 1 test and 0 validators
  • :| - 3 tests and 3 validators
  • :( - 1 test and 2 validators
  Please add more cases so the distribution is more balanced, with more :) and :( results. Also randomise the input strings to reduce long runs of the same emoticon appearing consecutively.

Thanks.
Sorry, I had realized I had missed some of the guidelines after I published it, and was updating when I received the notification for your comment. I'll try update the rest soon.

Thanks for catching those! I updated it. Is it fine, now?

Also, I may make a reverse-compatible one another time. It would probably have to find the mode, rather than the mean.
I’ve updated it to say “average happiness score.” Is that fine?

Yes; that helps a little.

It turns out I was confusing mean with majority vote, and that was why the test case above made no sense to me. Sorry about my brain fart; your description before was already solid enough. However, I think you should still keep your new description.
Thanks a lot!
I don’t know if I’m allowed to ask this, but if there’s nothing else, would you consider checking if you can approve the contribution?
Yes, looks alright to me. Thank you!
Sure, please let me know when you've finished, and I'll take another look.

I've updated the tests and validators so that each has the following:
:| 3 tests, 3 validators
:) 4 tests, 3 validators
:( 3 tests, 4 validators.

I don't think I miscounted, but in case I did, please let me know.

Thank you for updating the statement and the cases. They're better now, but a few further adjustments are still needed.

Goal
* Please update the statement according to my previous comment.

Input
* Please revise this section as follows:
<<Line 1:>> An integer [[n]] representing the number of emoticons.
<<Line 2:>> A string containing [[n]] space-separated emoticons.

Constraints
* Since [[n]] = 1 in some cases, please reduce the lower bound accordingly.

Cases
* The outputs are now balanced, but inputs are still somewhat unbalanced.

Current counts of :), :(, :|, (:, ):, |:
• tests: [25, 29, 7] vs [13, 8, 4]
• validators [27, 14, 5] vs [9, 15, 5]

Please modify the existing cases by replacing some of the left-eyed emoticons with the right-eyed versions to better balance the counts.

Thanks again.

Hi, sorry for the late reply.

I believe I have balanced the numbers, but I may have made errors in counting. Do you have any tips for future reference?

No worries.

Goal
* If I remember correctly, the Goal section previously included the task along with an explanation of how rounding should be performed. Please restore that part.

Cases
* Current counts of :), :(, :|, (:, ):, |: in inputs:
  • tests: [24, 23, 7] vs [14, 14, 4]
  • validators [6, 16, 1] vs [30, 13, 10]

Now, the left-eyed emoticons dominate the tests, while the right-eyed emoticons dominate the validators 😂. To balance the numbers, I suggest the following changes:

  • All that wait for this? (Validator 6): Change 3 |: to :|

  • PARTY! (Test 8): Change 5 :) to (:

  • All same? (Test 9): Change 4 :( to ):

  • You and I... (Validator 9): Change 12 (: to :)

Tips for future reference
* It's often easier to rely on randomisation, which naturally produces more balanced test cases. For example, for this puzzle you could start with two lists containing equal numbers of each emoticon (e.g. each variant appears 10 times in each list): one list for tests, and one for validators.

Optionally, if you want to hand-craft some specific cases, simply remove the required quantities of emoticons from the appropriate list.

After that, apply `random.shuffle` to each list, and split the shuffled lists to generate the remaining cases, while also ensuring that outputs are also balanced.

Thanks.

Thanks for the reference. I changed validator 6 and test 8 to your recommendation, but I let test and validator 9 be. I wanted them to all have the same direction of parenthesis. Should I just mirror them, by using right parentheses instead of left?

Side note: I had already tried randomizing them originally, but because of the mean average system rather than mode, they all tended to result in ":|".
Thank you for making the changes. The main imbalance now is in the validators, which use 6 :) and 30 (: overall. It'd be great if Validator 9 could be mirrored to use 13 (: and 3 ): .
Done, thanks! By the way, is my example solution fine, or should I have made it more uniform? I was intending to show different possible methods for solving, but that might be unnecessary.
That's really up to you; either approach is fine. 🙂
Thanks for all your help! I've published it for validation, now.
